availability.p: Published under a Attribution-NonCommercial-ShareAlike 4.0 International (CC BY-NC-SA 4.0)

editorialDecl.p: Encoding of this text has followed the recommendations of the LombardPress 1.0.0 guidelines for a diplomatic edition.

change.p: Created file for the first time.

div.head: Chapter 13 [Bnf 12926 Transcription]
div.p: Chapter 13.

¶ It remains to respond to the example that Sepúlveda brings forward from the sacred books — namely, from Deuteronomy ch. 7 and from the book of Joshua: where we read that God, on account of certain crimes, destroyed the seven nations who were inhabitants of the promised land. He contends moreover that this should be carried out against the Indians, citing also Saint Cyprian, whose meaning he inverts and adulterates, as he is accustomed to do with many other things.

First, therefore, most distinguished doctor Sepúlveda, I would like to know from you why God did not command the destruction of very many other idolaters who were living at that time. For with the exception of the Jewish people and the lineage that extended from Adam all the way to Christ, the whole world was given over to the worship of idols. Except for a few men noble in piety — Job, namely, and Melchizedek, and others whom God deigned to grant knowledge of the truth while living among the nations — as is evident from Genesis, Deuteronomy, and nearly all the books of the Old Testament.

And Paul and Barnabas bear witness in Acts ch. 14, saying: that God, namely, in past generations allowed all the nations to walk in their own ways — that is, their various rites and ceremonies of idols. This Ambrose treats at length in his two books on the calling of all nations, and all the histories of the pagans sufficiently attest to it.

Although, therefore, the whole world worshipped idols, God did not command that the Edomites or the Egyptians be destroyed by war — peoples who surpassed the other nations of the world in their foul worship of idols and in other crimes. On the contrary, God said: You shall not abhor the Edomite, for he is your brother. Nor Egypt, for you were a stranger in his land. Deuteronomy 27.

And so God by no means commanded the killing of any idolaters except those who inhabited the promised land, unless there happened to be some new cause apart from idolatry. This is proven from chapter 20 of that book, where God, instructing his people how they ought to conduct war against the pagans who dwelt outside the promised land — if perchance they should receive harm or injury from them, as Lyranus notes there and Abulensis in question 1 and 2 Chronicles ch. 8, q. 5 — admonished them with these words, saying:

If at any time you approach a city to besiege it, you shall first offer it peace. And further on: Thus you shall do to all the cities that are very far from you, and that are not among those cities which you are about to receive as your possession. But of those cities that shall be given to you, you shall permit no one at all to live, but shall put them to death by the edge of the sword and so forth.

And so against the Canaanites inhabiting the promised land, sufficient cause for war was simply that they were inhabitants of that land; therefore the Jews were bound by divine command to kill them, sparing neither great nor small, nor even the animals themselves at times.

Yet against other nations, even if they were impious and idolatrous, the Jews could not take up arms without some other legitimate cause; and in such cases they were bound first to offer them peace. If the others did not refuse it and therefore opened their gates, the Jews could afflict them with no other hardship than the imposition of tribute as a penalty for harm or injury inflicted upon themselves; and once tribute had been imposed, the others were left unharmed and free to inhabit their own cities.

But if they obstinately refused peace, persisting in that resolve, the Jews could, after taking the city, kill those who were fit for battle; yet they were bound to spare the women, children, and animals. Indeed they could even spare the men, for they were not bound by divine command to kill them, as Cajetan notes there.

And although this was indeed a general rule — namely, that no nation, however idolatrous, among those dwelling outside the promised land could be subjected to war or any other hardship unless some legitimate cause intervened — the Lord had, however, made two exceptions from all the others: namely the Midianites and the Amalekites, whom the Jews were bound by divine command to attack and destroy — not indeed because they were idolaters.

For, as has been said, they could not wage war against idolaters without a just cause, even if they were most impious; rather, it was because those peoples had inflicted certain particular wrongs upon the children of Israel. The Midianites were indeed the cause by which the children of Israel committed fornication with the women of that region and worshipped idols, and thereby the wrath of God came upon them; for which reason the Lord commanded them: let the Midianites feel that you are their enemies, and strike them, for they acted in a hostile manner against you. Numbers ch. 16.
The children of Israel could make war upon the Midianites and Amalekites.

The Amalekites, moreover, twice fought against the Jews in an unjust and impious manner in the desert: Exodus 17 and Numbers 14. Therefore God commanded them not to forget that injury, but to blot out the name of Amalek from the face of the earth as soon as they had first settled in their own land. Deuteronomy 26. And because Saul spared their king, he sinned gravely; for which reason the Lord did not permit his sons to succeed him in the kingdom. 1 Kings 15. The Lord, therefore, did not will that all idolaters be killed or subjected indiscriminately by war, unless some particular and just cause were present.

For a case that is admitted for a special reason does not make a general rule; on the contrary, it teaches that the common law is to the contrary, by the argument from the special provision — concerning which argument see l. quod vero contra and l. ius singulare. ff de legibus, and l. 1. ff ad municipia, and in the rule which is iure de regulis iuris, Book 69, with similar passages. These men therefore err, knowing neither the scriptures nor the power of God.

Nor does what they adduce from Lyranus on Numbers ch. 31 carry any more weight — where, teaching that certain just causes for which war was undertaken are found in the sacred books, he adds that a just cause for war is if the nation of some province blasphemes God by worshipping idols, Deuteronomy ch. 13. This indeed we do not deny, as it is read there. But that is understood to be true when they worship idols after having received the faith.

For if, having been warned and required to abstain from those impure sacrifices and sacrilegious worship, they refuse to do penance and to acknowledge the truth, war can justly be waged against them. This the Lord commanded through Moses, Deuteronomy 13. Although even those who had not been heard were bound to be destroyed at that time — concerning which see what is said in the following chapter. In this age of grace, however, something altogether different is to be thought and done (as I will demonstrate shortly).

The passages that Sepúlveda hammers in by constant repetition, so as to induce a lethargic sleep in those who pay no attention — Deuteronomy 12, 9, 18, and Leviticus 13, 18, 20 — speak of idolaters inhabiting the promised land, whose idols the Jews were bound to utterly demolish to their foundations. But to argue from idolaters inhabiting the promised land to other idolaters, especially after the coming of Christ, is absurd (as I have shown above); otherwise, if they press us with the authority of those passages, they will also teach that the Spaniards must necessarily kill the great and the small, the guilty and the innocent, even down to the beasts of burden, dogs, and cats — just as is commanded in some of those passages — which is most absurd.

But if they deny this — claiming that children, women, dogs, and beasts of burden ought to be spared — I will ask by what right they admit those passages with respect to men, yet reject them with respect to children, beasts of burden, and the other things commanded to be killed there? Is it for the sole reason that so it seems to Sepúlveda? He brings forward a sufficiently weak argument to deserve belief on this point. In sum, those passages speak of pagans inhabiting the promised land; against all others the Jews could not wage war without some other legitimate cause (as has been proven above many times).

I marvel, however, that this Christian man does not rather admit those passages which speak of pagans outside the promised land, so that we might use their analogy against all the nations of the world found to be given over to idolatry — since those passages are more closely in accord with the mildness of the evangelical teaching and the meekness and charity of Christ than those which rested upon that special and rigorous command.

¶ But so that the pernicious error and the ignorance — or certainly the malice — of certain adversaries, now exposed, may henceforth be more clearly manifest to all, as a further confirmation of what has been said, we wish to prove more fully that that rigorous command against the Canaanite nations was most special in character, and hence that those passages commanding idolaters to be killed are to be admitted only against the inhabitants of the promised land. This we prove by assigning certain reasons for this special character, drawn from the sacred scriptures.

Two of these reasons Saint Thomas assigns in book 4 of the Sentences, distinction 29, art. 1, ad 1m. First, because the wickedness of those nations was already complete, and therefore the Lord was unwilling to tolerate them any longer, given that they persisted in their sins with stubborn minds. For we read in Genesis 15 that God said to Abraham: to your seed I will give this land. And again, as if Abraham were asking the Lord why he would not give it to him at once, it is added: for the iniquities of the Amorites are not yet complete up to the present time. The iniquity of that nation, therefore, is believed to have been complete at the time when the children of Israel went out from the servitude of the Egyptians, at which point that command was imposed upon them to exterminate those peoples. This Gratian notes in c. remittuntur, 23 q. 5, § praescientes.

This obstinacy of the nation is expressly reported in the book of Joshua ch. 11: For it was the sentence of the Lord that their hearts should be hardened, and that they should not deserve any clemency.

¶ The second reason for which the Lord commanded the Hebrews in particular to kill those nations was on account of the danger that threatened the Hebrews themselves. For the Hebrews, as is clear from many passages of sacred scripture, were especially prone to idolatry. Lest therefore the proximity of the Canaanites, Amorites, and the other nations of that region draw them into the worship of idols, the Lord commanded those nations to be utterly exterminated: lest perhaps (he says) they teach you to do all the abominations which they have practiced for their gods, and you sin against the Lord your God. Deuteronomy 20 and 7.

For the same reason also the Lord commanded that they should not contract friendship or marriage with any of those nations. Deuteronomy 7. This Saint Thomas notes in the same place and in Ia IIae q. 105, art. 4, ad 6m. With the other idolatrous nations, however, the Jews were not prohibited from contracting friendship — especially if there was no danger present of their being drawn into their worship. Thus we see that it was permissible for the Hebrews to form marriage alliances with those peoples.

For Joseph took as wife the daughter of Potiphera, who was a priest of the idols of Egypt. Moses had an Ethiopian wife. Esther married King Ahasuerus. Solomon celebrated nuptials with the daughter of the king of Egypt. Indeed, even with idolaters very close to the promised land it was permissible to contract marriage. For we read in Ruth ch. 4 that Ruth the Moabite, born in the land of Moab which is very close to the land of Canaan, married Boaz.

Likewise in Deuteronomy ch. 21, the Lord permitted the Hebrews that if they should ever wage war against other enemies — that is, those not inhabiting the promised land — and conquer them, and among the captives should see a beautiful woman who appealed to them, they could, after performing certain rites, take her as a wife. Indeed they could even take as wives the daughters of pagans inhabiting the promised land, by dispensation. For Salmon, prince of the tribe of Judah, son of Nahshon, took as wife Rahab the harlot, who was born in the city of Jericho, which is in the inland region of Canaan — as we read in Numbers 12 and 71 and Matthew 1.

Third reason. ¶ In the Conferences of the Fathers, the conference of the holy Abbot Serapion, ch. 24, and Saint Epiphanius in the book entitled the Ancoratus, and Saint Augustine in sermon 105 de tempore offer another reason for this special command. Namely, because that region which the Canaanites possessed had fallen by lot to Shem, the eldest son of Noah, when Noah divided the world among his three sons. And an oath was exacted from them by their father that none of them should invade the lot of a brother, and that whoever transgressed this sworn precept should be exterminated by oath — he and his entire seed.

But after the sons of Shem had long possessed that region, the sons of Ham — whom Noah their father cursed (who are called Canaanites) — descending into that region, violently expelled the sons of Shem and took possession of their territory. Therefore the Lord, who is long-suffering, gave them time for repentance so that those born of Ham might repent and restore to the sons of Shem their proper inheritance; but they did not repent. Then God, being just after many generations, avenged those who had been wronged by the sons of Ham, exterminating the offspring of Ham in accordance with their oath, and the seed of Shem — that is, the children of Israel — received their own region. For thus it was necessary that the measure of the Amorites be fulfilled — that is, the wickedness of the Canaanites — both so that the kingdom might be restored to its lawful owners, and also so that the crimes of that nation might be punished.

¶ From this it clearly appears that the command of the Lord to exterminate the seven nations inhabiting the promised land had a particular cause, and that from it one cannot infer in general that pagan idolaters are to be killed — which we have proven from the passages of the Old Testament.

And since this was the case even in the time of the old law, which was harsh and severe, all the more is this to be understood today, when through the coming of Christ the Lord has poured out the treasures of his mercy over all the earth and over every nation. For this reason this time is called the time of grace, the time of love, the year of favor, the day of salvation and joy, the good news sent freely — as the prophets and both the Old and New Testaments are full of testimonies of this kind.

The venerable Doctor Sepúlveda therefore errs, who in this matter by no means carefully examined — or certainly did not understand well enough — the scriptures, wishing in this age of grace and mercy to put into practice the rigorous commands of the old law that were issued for a special reason, and thereby to open the way for tyrants and plunderers, under the pretext of religion, to cruelly invade, oppress, plunder, and reduce to bitter slavery innocent nations that have neither heard the faith nor could have suspected that the faith of Christ or the Christian religion existed in the nature of things.

What then? The examples of things that God at that time commanded to be done we admire, yet we ought not to imitate them — as is stated in c. nos si incompetenter, § his ita respondetur, 2 q. 7. For Samuel in one of those wars cut to pieces Agag, king of the Amalekites, 1 Kings 15; but now consecrated men abstain from all shedding of blood. Likewise Phinehas the priest killed a Jew who was lying with a Midianite woman; but now it would be judged impious for a priest to defile his hands with blood.

Furthermore the Hebrews then despoiled the Egyptians by a kind of stratagem, and the prophet Hosea took up with a harlot wishing to have children of fornication. But today, according to the evangelical law, if anyone did something similar he would be guilty of theft and licentiousness and would commit mortal sin. It was then permitted to have many wives; today one who did the same would be an adulterer.

Therefore Gregory in the Moralia, book 18, ch. 3, cited also in the Decretals, 22 q. 2, c. si quis, says: If anyone through the Old Testament wishes to defend his falsehood, because it perhaps harmed some people less there, let him say that the seizure of another's property and the requiting of injury — conceded to the weak there — cannot harm him. All of which, it is evident with what great correction truth pursues them, truth which now, having cast aside the shadow of its signification, is declared to us in true flesh.

And again at the end of ch. 2, Gregory says: For in the New Testament, with higher precepts, truth having been made manifest through the flesh, we make progress; and it is just that we abandon certain deeds which in that people served as a shadow of the truth. Thus Gregory.

Again, Augustine in his Questions on Exodus, read in 14 q. 5, c. dixit dominus, The Lord said (he writes) to Moses: let every man ask of his neighbor and every woman of her neighbor, vessels of silver and of gold and clothing — no one from this ought to think an example is being given for despoiling one's neighbor in this manner. For the Lord commanded this, who knew what each person ought to suffer, and the Israelites did not commit theft, but performed a service at God's command. Thus Augustine.

Those, therefore, who from those passages strive to prove that all unbelievers or idolaters in general are to be consumed by the sword of war — where of necessity so many homicides, so many plunderings, rapines, scandals, and infinitely many other irreparable evils and hardships intervene — on the grounds that God commanded the children of Israel to do such things to the Egyptians and Canaanites: must necessarily also confess that it is permissible to steal others' property, to avenge one's own injuries; they must also acknowledge what I have said above — that this would be nothing other than the practice of Judaism. But by the words of the Savior we are taught that we cannot attain salvation unless we surpass the justice of the Jews. Matthew 6.
